title: Do Credit Markets Respond to Macroeconomic Shocks? The Case for Reverse Causality
abstract: The response of corporate bond credit spreads to three exogenous macro shocks—oil supply, investment-specific technology, and government spending—is large, significant, and a mirror image of macroeconomic activity. This countercyclicality is driven largely by credit risk premia and translates into significant return predictability. Equity risk premia exhibit similar responses, providing external validity. Information rigidities and leverage play a key role in the transmission of the shocks. Since causal evidence linking macro shocks to credit markets is scarce and recent work highlights the real effects of credit fluctuations, our findings contribute to understanding the joint dynamics of credit markets and the macroeconomy.

article.introduction:
A long-standing goal of macrofinance is to disentangle the relation between credit markets and macroeconomic fluctuations. Recent empirical work shows that corporate bond credit spreads lead macroeconomic activity.1However, the reverse empirical relation—whether macroeconomic shocks lead credit spreads—has received little attention. The identification and transmission of macroeconomic shocks is a fundamental topic that has motivated a great number of papers, including those in which credit market frictions propagate and amplify primitive economic shocks.2The goal of this paper is to quantify the strength of “reverse causality” from macroeconomic shocks to credit markets and to study the underlying economic mechanisms.
  To understand the identification challenge, we show that measures of macroeconomic activity, such as gross domestic product (GDP) and the Chicago Fed National Activity Index (CFNAI), are negatively correlated with future values of the corporate bond spread of Gilchrist and Zakrajšek (2012, GZ) and the default spread between BAA and AAA bonds. Potential explanations for this reverse predictive correlation include a causal impact of macroeconomic conditions on credit markets, persistence in credit spreads coupled with a causal impact of credit markets on macroeconomic conditions, and, both macroeconomic activity and credit spreads being affected by another common source, such as frictions in credit markets or sentiment.
  To address this identification challenge, we use macroeconomic shocks as instruments to determine whether macroeconomic conditions cause, in a structural sense, fluctuations in credit spreads. As surveyed in Ramey (2016) and Stock and Watson (2016), the literature has made significant progress in identifying primitive shocks that cause fluctuations in macroeconomic activity. We consider three such shocks that are large, infrequent, and plausibly exogenous to current and past credit market conditions: oil supply (Baumeister and Hamilton (2019)), investment-specific technology (IST) news (Ben Zeev and Khan (2015)), and defense spending news (Ramey (2011)).3Under the assumption that oil supply restrictions due to the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries' (OPEC) oil embargoes and conflicts in the Middle East, news of technology-related productivity shocks, and of government spending increases due to political events and significant military buildups are exogenous, we can interpret the impact of these shocks on credit markets as a consequence of their impact on macroeconomic activity.4
  Using the local linear projection approach of Jordà (2005), we find that an unexpected increase in oil supply, IST news, and government spending news leads to a significant and delayed reduction in credit spreads. For instance, six quarters after a positive two-standard-deviation oil supply shock, the GZ spread dips 40 basis points (bps) below its mean, or about 42% of its unconditional standard deviation. For each of the three shocks, the impulse response of credit spreads is similar in magnitude but opposite in sign to the response of macroeconomic activity. Furthermore, credit spreads trough at the same time as or later than the peak in macroeconomic activity. The fact that the responses of credit spreads and macroeconomic activity are close to mirror images is not imposed by our method and confirms that credit spreads respond countercyclically to macroeconomic shocks.
  Importantly, we find that the shocks are major determinants of fluctuations in credit spreads and real activity. Using the forecast error variance decompositions (FEVD) tailored to local projections (see Gorodnichenko and Lee (2020), Plagborg-Moller and Wolf (2022)), we show that the three shocks together explain 30% to 50% of the unpredictable variation in credit spreads and around 40% of the unpredictable variation in macroeconomic activity at horizons beyond one year. The fact that the shocks are diverse in origin, motivation, and sample period is a strong validation of our identification strategy and speaks to the robustness of our findings.
  Our results raise important questions related to the transmission mechanism of the shocks. First, if we decompose credit spreads into a default component due to credit losses (cash flows) and credit risk premia (discount rates), how do these individual components respond to macro shocks? Second, why do we observe a protracted response of credit spreads? Under the null of full-information rational expectations (FIRE), bond prices should respond immediately and fully when a shock hits. Could the delayed response be due to information rigidities that lead to expectations and price dynamics different from those under the null? We answer these questions in turn.
  To answer the first question, we present results for two alternative decompositions. The approach in Nozawa (2017) is analogous to the standard Campbell and Shiller (1988a,1988b) decomposition of dividend yields and requires the estimation of a vector autoregression (VAR) that links the predictability in corporate bond returns (in excess of a matching Treasury bond return) to the credit spread through a log-linear approximation. This decomposition assigns most of the variation in aggregate credit spreads to time-varying discount rates (credit risk premia) rather than credit losses because shocks to the latter are more idiosyncratic across firms.5The decomposition in GZ defines as an “excess bond premium” the equal-weighted average residual from a projection of corporate bond yield spreads on Merton's distance-to-default, which represents the default component. The most important difference between the Nozawa (2017) and GZ approaches is that the latter attributes to the default component any variation in risk premia that coincides with systematic variation in the distance-to-default.
  While the two decompositions are different in nature, they yield qualitatively similar results: the response of the discount rate component accounts for most of the countercyclical response of credit spreads. For instance, following a positive two-standard-deviation oil supply shock, the discount rate component in the decomposition of Nozawa (2017) dips about 1.3% below its mean, which translates to about two-thirds of the trough response of the not-decomposed credit spread. For the case of IST news, the relative contribution of the discount rate component at the trough is even larger at over 90%. These relative contributions are only slightly smaller (at 50% and 85%, respectively) in the decomposition of GZ. We therefore conclude that credit risk premia are strongly time-varying and contribute more than defaults to the response of credit spreads to macro shocks.
  A natural concern is whether the decompositions correctly identify expected corporate bond returns, which are inherently unobservable. We alternatively proxy for risk premia by running time-series predictive regressions of realized corporate bond returns on two sets of predictors: macro variables and credit spreads. These “first-stage” regressions suggest that corporate bond returns are predictable, withR2s of around 0.20 at annual frequency. Consistent with the idea that macro variables and credit spreads are ultimately driven by the same macroeconomic shocks, these two sets of predictors generate similar time series of implied bond risk premia (fitted from the predictive regressions).
  In a second stage, we estimate the response of the fitted risk premia to the exogenous shocks. We find that the impact of the shocks on macro variables and credit spreads aggregates to a significant negative impact on expected corporate bond market returns (as well as expected returns of long-short portfolios sorted on various bond characteristics). Expected corporate bond market returns fall by almost 2% in the two years after a two-standard-deviation oil supply or IST news shock, which is large relative to an unconditional average return of 3.5%. These results contribute to the existing literature that finds only a small role for macro variables in explaining time-variation in credit spreads and expected corporate bond returns, especially when compared to variables related to illiquidity (e.g., Collin-Dufresne, Goldstein, and Martin (2001), Elton et al. (2001), Longstaff, Mithal, and Neis (2005)) and sentiment (Greenwood and Hanson (2013), López-Salido, Stein, and Zakrajšek (2017)). Our results are different because we are the first to study the causal impact of exogenous macro shocks on credit spreads and proxies for credit risk premia.
  To answer the second question (i.e., why do we observe a protracted response of credit spreads), we explore various mechanisms that might explain the delay and amplification of the shocks. A natural starting point is to test the predictions of rational expectations models with information frictions, such as sticky information or noisy information.6Intuitively, credit spreads may not fully respond when shocks hit because the exact information content is not immediately reflected in market participants' expectations. Coibion and Gorodnichenko (2012,2015) show more formally that, for a broad set of models with information rigidities, macroeconomic and financial quantities respond gradually to shocks and, more importantly, market participants' expectations respond with further delay. As a result, the difference between realized quantities and expectations is predictable under information rigidity. Based on this insight, Coibion and Gorodnichenko (2012,2015) derive general tests for the null of FIRE and find support for the alternative of information rigidity in consensus forecasts of inflation and other macroeconomic quantities.
  Adapting the approach of Coibion and Gorodnichenko (2012) to our setting, we find support for information rigidity in credit markets. Market participants' forecasts of credit spreads respond with the same sign and magnitude as realized credit spreads to shocks, but the forecasts lag the realizations by a few quarters. Consequently, forecast errors respond with the same sign as the actual variable and significantly so. These results obtain for two different forecasts of credit spreads, namely, forecasts from the Survey of Professional Forecasters (SPF) and Blue Chip forecasts, respectively. While corporate bond market illiquidity driven by over-the-counter (OTC) market frictions can also explain a delayed price response to news, such frictions cannot explain the predictability of forecast errors. Moreover, we find that financial intermediary balance sheet capacity, which Goldberg and Nozawa (2021) argue is a key determinant of bond market liquidity, does not respond strongly to the shocks that we study nor does it lead credit spreads. We therefore conclude that information rigidity is key to understanding the slow movement of credit risk premia after macro shocks.
  We also explore potential amplification mechanisms linked to the quantity of credit, namely, fluctuations in firm leverage and debt issuance. Gomes and Schmid (2021) show that in a general equilibrium setting, macroeconomic fluctuations lead to endogenous fluctuations in leverage and in risk premia on stocks and corporate debt. This model, like others in the literature, predicts that the response to macro shocks is most pronounced for high-leverage firms with the most risky debt. We confirm this prediction in the data: the response of credit spreads is roughly twice as large for bonds of firms with high versus low leverage and for speculative-grade versus investment-grade bonds. We find no evidence to support the idea that debt issuance responds to macro shocks, which is perhaps unsurprising because issuance is used in recent literature as the natural outcome of a boom in sentiment rather than a response to real shocks.
  A compelling theoretical argument suggests that expected returns share a common time-varying component across asset classes (Cochrane (2011)). Since the shocks we consider are macro and affect the entire economy, it is reasonable to expect that they also lead variation in the expected returns of assets other than corporate bonds. Turning to equity markets, we find that the response to macro shocks of various proxies for equity risk premia—including the dividend yield, risk aversion implied by the habit model of Campbell and Cochrane (1999), and the news-VIX of Manela and Moreira (2017)—is qualitatively and quantitatively similar to the response of credit spreads.
  This finding is of significance because it (i) provides external validity to our hypothesis that negative macro shocks cause a significant increase in risk premia and (ii) establishes a common economic source of variation in discount rates of equities and credits. This commonality has so far eluded empirical finance because the correlation between the most popular proxy for equity risk premia—the dividend yield—and credit spreads is low in the data relative to what is implied by theory (e.g., Chen, Collin-Dufresne, and Goldstein (2009), Gomes and Schmid (2021)). A variety of confounding factors may contribute to this unconditional disconnect (sentiment, payout policy, flight-to-liquidity, among others). The shocks we study hold these factors constant and thus provide for a powerful empirical test of the hypothesis that time-variation in risk premia in stock and bond markets is common. In this way, our work complements recent work on the joint macroeconomic drivers of risk premia in equities and Treasuries (e.g., Koijen, Lustig, and Nieuwerburgh (2017), Campbell, Pflueger, and Viceira (2020)).
  This paper contributes to the literature on credit markets and macroeconomic activity. This literature has produced a list of credit boom indicators that forecast macroeconomic activity.7This direction of causality is well established. In contrast, we study the reverse direction of causality using exogenous shocks as macroeconomic fluctuations. Because of their origin, macro shocks are unlikely to be correlated with proxies for financial frictions, time-variation in sentiment, reaching-for-yield behavior, and credit supply expansions, although they might affect future values of these quantities. We find that positive macroeconomic shocks affect credit markets by improving investment prospects in the economy and ultimately reducing expected bond returns, which is consistent with the evidence in Keim and Stambaugh (1986), Fama and French (1989), and the more recent work of Gomes, Grotteria, and Wachter (2019). We contribute to this literature by providing direct identification of the time-variation in investment opportunities: the macroeconomic shocks.
  A related literature seeks to quantify the impact of monetary policy shocks on credit markets.8In recent work, Brunnermeier et al. (2021) study the endogenous relation between monetary policy, credit conditions, and output in a structural VAR to determine what causes the negative reduced-form responses of output to credit conditions. We instead study the impact of nonmonetary shocks on credit spreads and decompose the response of credit spreads to investigate the asset pricing implications. We thus expand the spectrum of macroeconomic disturbances that impact credit conditions and provide new moments that models studying the two-way interaction between the macroeconomy and credit markets should target. Indeed, our results imply feedback from the macroeconomy to credit conditions and are therefore also useful to understand the propagation of credit shocks. Our results on government spending shocks and corporate bond credit spreads are consistent with Auerbach, Gorodnichenko, and Murphy (2020), who document that positive fiscal policy shocks, in the form of Department of Defense contracts, lower consumer borrowing rates. While Auerbach, Gorodnichenko, and Murphy (2020) conclude that lower consumer credit risk premia “possibly” contribute to this response, we quantify the exact contribution of credit risk premia. Finally, the causal effect of IST news on credit spreads contributes to a growing body of work that emphasizes the importance of investment-specific technology shocks in asset pricing (Kogan and Papanikolaou (2013,2014)) and macroeconomics (Fisher (2006), Ben Zeev and Khan (2015)).
  The rest of the paper is structured as follows. In SectionI, we describe the data and identification challenge. In SectionII, we estimate the countercyclical response of credit spreads to macro shocks and quantify the fraction of spread variation that is attributable to these shocks. In SectionIII, we decompose the response of credit spreads into cash flow and discount rate components using two methods and show that macro shocks operate largely through discount rates. In SectionIV, we argue that information rigidities and leverage account for the prolonged and large effect of macro shocks on credit spreads. In SectionV, we study the response of commonly used proxies for expected stock returns to macro shocks. SectionVIconcludes.
article.I. Data and Motivation:
Our first goal in this paper is to study the response of credit spreads,, to shocks in macroeconomic activity,, over both a short and a long historical sample. For the shorter sample period, which starts in 1973Q1 as dictated by the availability of corporate bond data, we study the link between the CFNAI index (CFNAI, available from 1967Q2) and the GZ credit spread (GZ, available from 1973Q1).CFNAIis a popular indicator of growth among practitioners and academics (e.g., Joslin, Priebsch, and Singleton (2014), Koijen, Lustig, and Nieuwerburgh (2017)).GZis given as the equal-weighted average yield spread between a corporate bond and a synthetically matched Treasury bond. Because the latter measure uses a large cross section of bonds and the matching cleans up variation in corporate bond yields that is unrelated to credit risk (like term risk), it is a strong predictor of macroeconomic activity relative to other credit spread proxies (GZ, Faust et al. (2013)).
  The long historical sample uses almost a century of data starting from 1919Q1. This sample period is dictated by the availability of a popular alternative measure of credit spreads, the default spread between the yield of BAA- and AAA-rated corporate bonds (DS, e.g., Fama and French (1989), López-Salido, Stein, and Zakrajšek (2017)). Macroeconomic activity over the long sample is measured by GDP, detrended as in Ramey and Zubairy (2018) and denoted by. We plot the credit spreads and macro variables in FigureIA.1in SectionIV.Aof theInternet Appendix.9Over the common sample period, the correlation betweenand(and) is 0.32 (0.19).

Current credit spreads also correlate negatively witha few quarters ahead. To address this identification challenge and determine the causal impact of macroeconomic activity on credit spreads, in the next section we make plausible identification assumptions.
article.II. Do Macro Shocks Lead Credit Spreads?:
Our starting point in this section is a structural VAR that requires relatively strong identification assumptions on the contemporaneous relation between macroeconomic activity and credit spreads. We then turn to an exercise that requires much weaker identification assumptions using exogenous macro shocks.
  Figure2plots the impulse response function of credit spreads to a shock to macroeconomic activity,, with 90% bootstrapped confidence intervals.11
  The size of the shock is one-standard-deviation of the reduced-form residual,. We find that credit spreads respond negatively and significantly to the macro shocks. For all three combinations ofand, the response oftroughs at most two quarters after the shock toat about −0.15 (for the model withandand the model withand, respectively) and −0.27 (for the model withand). The response ofto the-shock is negative and significant for about three quarters, whereas the response ofto shocks toandremains negative and significant up to almost four years ahead. Quantitatively, the evidence implies that a shock that movesfrom its 90thto its 10thpercentile leads to an increase of 42 and 36 bps, respectively, inandnext quarter. These responses are economically large, given a quarterly standard deviation of 96 and 44 bps forand, respectively. In fact, the cumulative impact over the first year after such a macro shock is about 1.5% for bothand.
  To summarize, under the identification assumption of GZ that macroeconomic activity responds to credit spreads only with a lag, macro shocks have a significantly negative effect on future credit spreads. The countercyclical response of credit spreads is economically large and takes at least two years to fully mean-revert. Furthermore, the fact that the contemporaneous impact of macro shocks on credit spreads is negative implies that innovations in credit spreads, which are close to the inverse of returns, are negatively correlated with the macro shocks. The evidence therefore indicates that corporate bond returns are positively exposed to macroeconomic risk. In SectionIII, we examine whether this macroeconomic risk is compensated with a time-varying risk premium.
  In this section, we use an alternative method to trace out the impact of macro shocks on credit spreads. Rather than identifying structural shocks by imposing short-horizon restrictions in a VAR, rely on to a literature that has identified various primitive shocks that explain a significant fraction of future macroeconomic fluctuations (see Ramey (2016) for a review). We focus on the following three shocks: oil supply from Baumeister and Hamilton (2019), IST news from Ben Zeev and Khan (2015), and defense spending news from Ramey (2011). We choose these shocks as they are plausibly exogenous to current and lagged conditions in the credit market. Below, we provide more detail about each individual shock and discuss robustness to alternative shocks.
  For the sake of comparison and reproducibility, we download the time series of the shocks from the authors' websites. Formally, the shocks are “external instruments,” because they are not obtained from the VAR ofin equation (2). The identifying assumption here is weaker than in the structural VAR: identification only requires that the instrumenting shocks are unanticipated and are correlated with the “true” but unobservable structural shock to the macroeconomy (Stock and Watson (2016)).
  To preview some of the results, we confirm the finding in previous literature that the shocksare relevant instruments—each shock significantly impacts future macroeconomic activity. However, the local projections do capture important differences between the magnitude and timing of each shock's impact. For instance, the response of GDP to the defense spending shock peaks at around three years, whereas the response of GDP to the IST shock peaks at around one year. In addition, the shocks are available over different sample periods. The defense spending shocks are available since the late 1800s and are largest around the second world war. In contrast, the sample of oil supply shocks starts only in 1975, and these shocks are largest in the 1980s. In other words, the impulse responses from equations (3) and (4) are estimated with shocks of different nature and across different sample periods. Requiring a consistent response of credit spreads to three different macro shocks raises the bar considerably for us to claim that macro shocks lead credit spreads and therefore represents a strength of our empirical approach. We now discuss results for each shock in more detail.
  Oil Supply: Hamilton (1983) argues that oil supply shocks are a major determinant of economic fluctuations. This argument has inspired a large literature studying the macroeconomic effects of oil shocks. A major point of debate is how to disentangle the oil price impact of shocks to supply versus demand (e.g., Kilian (2009), Kilian and Murphy (2014), Baumeister and Hamilton (2019), Caldara, Cavallo, and Iacoviello (2019)). For our analysis, we use the oil supply shocks of Baumeister and Hamilton (2019). These shocks are extracted from a Bayesian VAR that relaxes the strong assumption in prior literature that there is no short-run response of oil supply or production to the oil price. Moreover, the setup of Baumeister and Hamilton (2019) allows them to use more data than in previous work (both a longer time series and additional information on supply and demand elasticities). The sample period is 1975Q1 to 2017Q4 and the largest shocks occur during the uncertainty about OPEC production in 1986 and at the start of the Gulf war in 1990Q3.

The response ofremains positive and significant from about four to nine quarters ahead. The results are intuitive, as a positive shock to oil supply is indeed good news for the economy.
  In the bottom panel (Panel E), we see that the response ofis almost a mirror image of the response of. We conclude that credit spreads respond strongly to oil supply shocks with a lag, with this response opposite to the response of macroeconomic activity. Furthermore, there is no evidence to suggest that credit spreads respond before macroeconomic activity, which is surprising given the forward-looking nature of asset prices.
  IST News: IST is a measure of the quality-adjusted price of new capital goods. Ramey (2016) argues that IST shocks are among the most important types of nonpolicy shocks and that these shocks are also plausibly exogenous to credit markets. Our starting point is the unanticipated shock to IST of Ben Zeev and Khan (2015), which the authors decompose into (i) the contemporaneous unanticipated shock to IST and (ii) news about future IST that is uncorrelated to (i). Whereas previous literature focuses on shocks to IST that are not decomposed (e.g., Kogan and Papanikolaou (2013,2014) for applications in finance), Ramey (2016, p. 151) writes that the IST news shock (ii) is “… promising both for generating business-cycle comovement and for contributing significant amounts to the variance of output.” In a robustness check, we find consistent evidence using a popular alternative productivity shock, namely, the Total Factor Productivity (TFP) news shock of Kurmann and Otrok (2013).

However, for IST shocks, the response of credit spreads is delayed relative to macroeconomic activity.   Defense Spending News: We next consider unexpected fluctuations in government spending due to defense spending news as proposed in Ramey (2011). This news series is constructed using narrative evidence and captures changes in the expected present discounted value of government spending that are linked to political and military events. Defense spending news shocks are a particularly suitable instrument for our purposes as, given they are driven by major military conflicts and wars, they are likely to be exogenous to the state of the U.S. economy and credit markets. Ramey and Zubairy (2018) find that the response of GDP to these unexpected government spending shocks (i.e., the government spending multiplier) is significant but lower than one. Data for these shocks are available since the late 1800s, but the shocks are relatively large only around wars and major unexpected military buildups. Ramey (2011) therefore argues that defense spending news is a weak instrument for macroeconomic activity for the sample period after the Korean War (1950 to 1953). Accordingly, we (i) focus on the impulse responses ofandto the defense spending news shock (as both variables are available over a common sample from 1919Q1 onward) and (ii) present conditional results for a sample over which the defense spending news instrument is relatively strong (from 1935Q1 to 1959Q4, thus including large shocks around World War II and the Korean War). Results for alternative measures of macroeconomic activity are similar and presented in FigureIA.6.
  The third column of Figure3displays the estimated impulse responses following a one-standard-deviation defense spending news shock. We find that these shocks have a large, immediate, and lasting impact on GDP: the response is positive and remains so up to four years out, consistent with Ramey and Zubairy (2018). The peak impact is about 0.16 standard deviations above the mean at the 2.5-year horizon. The impact of defense spending shocks on credit spreads is negative. The response ofis small and insignificant up to about two years out, after which the response turns significantly negative, peaking at about −0.07 standard deviations at the four-year horizon. Quantitatively, these estimates imply thatincreases by about 10 bps in the years after a negative two-standard-deviation defense spending shock. Finally, defense spending news is much more important for credit spreads when we condition on the instrument being strong. In the sample from 1935Q1 to 1959Q4, the negative impact of the defense spending news shock onsimilarly builds over time, but it is almost twice as large and significant for all horizons longer than one year. In conclusion, macroeconomic activity and credit spreads respond with opposite sign to defense spending news, just as they do in response to oil supply shocks and IST news. For defense spending news, the response of credit spreads also occurs with substantial delay relative to the response of macroeconomic activity.
  The impulse response functionsandin equations (3) and (4) capture the impact of the shocks on future credit spreads. However, they do not speak to how important the shocks are quantitatively for capturing the historical variation in credit spreads. To answer this question, we follow recent work by Gorodnichenko and Lee (2020) and Plagborg-Moller and Wolf (2022) and assess the contribution of the macro shocks to the variation in credit spread forecast errors at different horizons. Specifically, we apply the FEVD approach of Gorodnichenko and Lee (2020) because it is uniquely suited to perform a variance decomposition for the local projections that we study. We briefly describe their two-step approach applied to our setting.
  Oil Supply: In the first column of Figure4, we see that the oil supply shock explains an economically large share of the forecast error variance of both macroeconomic activity and credit spreads. We see that in both cases the variance contribution is significant from horizons of about one to three years and peaks 2.5 years out at 18% in the case ofand 21% in the case of. Thus, about one-fifth of the unpredictable variation in macroeconomic activity and credit spreads is captured by oil shocks in the medium term. To put this contribution into perspective, note that monetary policy shocks typically explain less than 10% of the variation in macro aggregates (see GZ (p. 1712) and Ramey (2016, Section 3)).
  IST News: In the second column of Figure4, we note that IST news explains a similarly large and significant share of the unpredictable variation in macroeconomic activity and credit spreads. For credit spreads, the contribution to the forecast error variance peaks at a significant 15% five quarters after the shock. FigureIA.7shows that the TFP news shock of Kurmann and Otrok (2013) explains even slightly larger shares of the variation inand. For this alternative productivity shock, the peak contribution occurs slightly later (at the two- to three-year horizon).
  Defense Spending News: In the third column of Figure4, defense spending news explains a significant share of the variation in growth over the full sample, but not that in credit spreads. This finding is consistent with the smaller impact that this shock has on credit spreads relative to its impact on growth (and also relative to the impact of oil supply and productivity shocks on credit spreads). That said, the defense spending news shock is much more important for credit spreads when we condition on the period when this instrument is strong (last column). From 1935Q1 to 1959Q4, the shock explains as much as 50% of the unpredictable variation in credit spreads at long horizons.
  Joint Contribution of Macro Shocks: Given the oil supply, IST news, and defense spending news shocks are different in nature and hit at different times, we now examine their joint contribution to the variation in credit spread forecast errors. To do so, we include all three shocks inon the right-hand side of equations (7) to (8) and proceed analogously to the single-shock case for the computation of the bias adjustment and confidence intervals using bootstrap.14Figure5plots the joint contribution of the three macro shocks to the forecast error variance ofand. We find that the shocks explain similarly large and significant shares of the variation in macroeconomic activity and credit spreads. For, the contribution is around 40% at horizons beyond one year. For, the contribution of the macro shocks is 30% to 50% at similar horizons. We note that these numbers are quite consistent in magnitude with summing the individual contributions for each shock from Figure4. This simple arithmetic is correct when the shocks are uncorrelated, which again underscores why studying these three shocks is of interest.
  Taken together, the impulse responses and variance decompositions above convey a remarkably clear message. Oil supply, IST news, and defense spending news shocks all lead to a sizeable response in credit spreads and account for a significant fraction of its variation. The consistency in responses and variance decompositions is particularly noteworthy because the underlying economic motivation for the shocks is so diverse. Moreover, the shocks differ in terms of construction, identification, time-series properties, and sample period. Hence, the homogeneity of the results suggests that a causal impact of macro shocks on credit markets is a robust and quantitatively important feature of the data. This conclusion does not imply that all shocks extracted from macroeconomic data lead credit spreads: relevant shocks are those with sizeable impact on the macroeconomy. For instance, FigureIA.8shows that credit spreads do not respond strongly to the oil demand shocks constructed in Baumeister and Hamilton (2019), as these shocks have little to no impact on macroeconomic activity as well.
  We also note that the response of credit spreads to each shock is close to a mirror image of the response of macroeconomic activity. Indeed, credit spreads respond with a lag to each shock and with opposite sign to macroeconomic activity. Although the peak response occurs at a different horizon for each shock, it is similar in absolute magnitude. The overall shape of the impulse responses suggests that credit spreads respond with about the same delay as macro variables, which is surprising. Under full-information rational expectations, forward-looking corporate bond prices should to respond fully and immediately to the information in the shock. This is not what we find. We dig deeper into the rejection of this fundamental hypothesis in SectionIV.
  Although a causal impact of macro shocks is assumed in the credit channel literature (Bernanke and Gertler (1995), Bernanke, Gertler, and Gilchrist (1999)), to date such effect has not been quantified empirically to the extent that we do. Our FEVD findings that macro shocks explain between 30% and 50% of the variation in credit spreads suggest that the impact of these shocks on credit markets is large. Nevertheless, this impact is largely ignored in recent studies that focus on the relation between current credit spreads and future macroeconomic activity. Furthermore, these studies tend to find that credit shocks (broadly defined) explain considerably smaller fractions of the variation in macro aggregates. For instance, in the VAR of GZ, the variance contribution of excess bond premium-shocks to GDP is at most 12% (see FigureIA.9and theInternet Appendixfor a detailed discussion).
  Our results are also difficult to reconcile with the popular narrative that credit market sentiment drives economic fluctuations, unless one is able to tie sentiment to oil supply, productivity, and defense spending shocks. Indeed, these external shocks do not require a shift in sentiment to explain the response of credit spreads. The response of credit spreads may simply reflect a change in risk premia commensurate with the impact of the shock on economic conditions. We test this hypothesis in the following section.
article.III. Discount Rate and Cash Flow Compone:
Which component of credit spreads contributes most to the response to macro shocks? Conceptually, credit spreads increase when the discount rate of corporate debt rises, corporate bonds' default risk increases, or both.
  Existing evidence suggests that credit spreads are driven to a large extent by factors that are unrelated to credit fundamentals (defaults).15If not credit fundamentals, what are the drivers of time-variation in credit spreads and credit risk premia? Existing empirical work points to an important role for illiquidity (e.g., Collin-Dufresne, Goldstein, and Martin (2001), Elton et al. (2001), Longstaff, Mithal, and Neis (2005)) and variables related to investor sentiment (Greenwood and Hanson (2013), López-Salido, Stein, and Zakrajšek (2017)). Although first principles suggest an important role for macroeconomic risk because credit losses cluster in recessions, evidence supporting this effect is relatively scarce.
  Expected corporate bond returns and expected defaults are ultimately unobservable. Therefore, in this section we decompose the time series of credit spreads into a cash flow component that captures credit fundamentals and a remaining discount rate component. We use the decompositions of GZ and Nozawa (2017), which differ in their specification, data, and identifying assumptions. Once the cash flow and discount rate components are identified, we reestimate responses with local linear projections for each component to establish the source of fluctuations in credit spreads. We also proxy for risk premia using realized corporate bond returns. Because the individual corporate bond data needed for these exercises are available only from 1973 onward, in this section we focus on oil supply and IST news shocks. As discussed before, defense spending news is a weak instrument for macroeconomic activity over this sample period.

article.IV. Exploring the Economic Mechanisms:
In this section, we investigate how macro shocks are transmitted into credit spreads. First, we analyze what may cause the protracted response of credit spreads and credit risk premia to macro shocks. To do so, we appeal to the insight that prices and risk premia are driven by agents' expectations and explore how expectations in credit markets respond to macro shocks. Second, we explore potential transmission through fluctuations in firm leverage, intermediaries' balance sheets, and debt issuance, which feature prominently in recent literature on credit markets.
  Under the FIRE, asset prices respond immediately to a shock. Under information rigidity, the response will be delayed due to various information frictions.23The most prominent models of such frictions are sticky information models, in which inattentive agents update their information sets infrequently (e.g., Mankiw and Reis (2002)), and noisy information models, in which agents update their beliefs only gradually in response to shocks to fundamentals because their signals are imperfect (e.g., Sims (2003), Mackowiak and Wiederholt (2009)). Recent papers on information frictions focus on agents' learning about the true model or future fundamentals (e.g., Giacoletti, Laursen, and Singleton (2021), Farmer, Nakamura, and Steinsson (2022)). Coibion and Gorodnichenko (2012,2015) test for deviations from the FIRE and find evidence of information rigidities in consensus forecasts of inflation and other macroeconomic quantities.
  Under the null that market participants' forecasts are formed by the FIRE, the forecast errors should be unforecastable. The key insight from Coibion and Gorodnichenko (2012) is that models with information rigidities instead predict that forecasts underreact to news, which implies a significant response of mean forecast errors across agents to economic shocksandwith the same sign as the response of. In other words, if we run the projections in equations (10) and (11) for GDP, we expectandunder information rigidity. Analogously, we expect for credit spreadsandunder the information rigidity hypothesis.
  Let us describe the data we use in these tests. Forecasts of (real per-capita) GDP growth come from the SPF and are available since 1968Q4. Our first proxy for the credit spread is the difference between Moody's AAA corporate bond yield and the 10-year Treasury bond rate, for which forecasts are available from the SPF since 1992Q1. This proxy is different from the BAA-AAA spread () that we use throughout our paper because forecasts for BAA-rated bonds are available from SPF only since 2010Q1. Our second proxy is the difference between BAA- and AAA-rated bonds, for which forecasts are available from Blue Chip since 1999Q1. To accommodate the longer samples over which we observe the shocks, we extend the sample for this proxy backward by projecting the BAA-AAA spread (and its forecast) on the spread between AAA and 10-year Treasury bonds (and its forecast, also from Blue Chip). We thus end up with a sample for the Blue Chip-based credit spread proxy starting in 1993Q1. We use oil supply and IST news shocks for these tests because defense spending news is a weak instrument over the period when we observe forecasts. Finally, we consider horizons up tobecause it is unlikely that forecasts underreact to a shock that happened more than two years prior, especially when forecasters have already seen most of the shock's economic impact by that point. This choice also accommodates the level of information rigidity in macro quantities estimated in previous literature. For instance, Coibion and Gorodnichenko (2015) find that forecasters update their information for predicting inflation every six to seven months.
  We present the results in Figures9and10. Consistent with information rigidity, we uncover two main findings that hold equally for GDP growth and credit spreads. First, forecasts of GDP and credit spreads respond with the same sign and similar magnitude to the realization of these variables, but the peaks (in the case of GDP) and troughs (in case of credit spreads) are delayed by a few quarters for the forecast relative to the realization. Second, and consequently, forecast errors respond with the same sign as the actual variable (and) and significantly so.
  Let us first consider the oil supply shock. Panel A of Figure9shows that GDP peaks at 44 bps above the meanquarters after the shock. The forecast of GDP peaks only a quarter later and at a lower value of 16 bps. Combining these effects, Panel B shows that the forecast error is positive for the first years after the shock and peaksquarters out at a statistically significant 35 bps. This response is economically significant as well, as it translates to roughly one-sixth of a standard deviation in realized GDP. Similarly, we see in Panel C of Figure9that the realized credit spread starts to decrease immediately when the shock hits, whereas the SPF-based credit spread forecast actually increases over the first quarter. Furthermore, we estimate that the realized credit spread falls −0.032% below the mean at, whereas at that point the forecast is still a positive 0.015%. Panel D shows that the difference, that is, the forecast error, is negative over the first years after the shock. The forecast error troughs atat a significant −0.046%, which translates to roughly one-eighth of a standard deviation in the credit spread. In Panel F, we see that the Blue Chip-based credit spread forecast error is also negative after the shock and troughs at an even larger −0.077%quarters after the shock, which translates to about 0.2 standard deviations in the realized credit spread. This response is estimated less precisely, however.
  Figure10shows broadly similar patterns for IST news, with only one major difference: realized GDP and credit spreads respond relatively faster to this shock. Forecasts still respond with a delay, however. For instance, forecasts of credit spreads (Panels C and E) trough two to three quarters after the realized credit spread, such that the forecast error is negative over the first year after the shock. For the SPF-based forecast, the forecast error troughs at a significant −0.068% at(Panel D); for the Blue Chip-based forecast, the forecast error is significant at horizonsand troughs at −0.152% at(Panel F).
  In conclusion, our evidence is consistent with the presence of information rigidities in credit markets. As noted in Coibion and Gorodnichenko (2012,2015), this evidence is particularly notable because the professional forecasters included in the SPF and Blue Chip surveys are among the most informed economic agents. While information rigidity is well known to play a role in the assessment of macroeconomic news, we show that the same mechanism is important to understand the protracted response of credit spreads to macro shocks. We thus argue that credit spreads, and asset prices more generally, do not respond fully when shocks hit because the exact content of this information is not immediately clear to the market. Importantly, the major types of models on information rigidity have this same prediction, so our results do not allow us to differentiate between them. That said, an interesting avenue for future work is to understand which type of information rigidity is key to generate our results.
  Leverage: It is well established that balance sheet factors such as leverage and creditworthiness amplify aggregate shocks because of credit market frictions.25Consistent with this amplification prediction, the response of credit spreads to macro shocks should be most pronounced for bonds of highly leveraged companies. To test this implication, we sort bonds in each month into high, mid, and low leverage (see SectionIV.Mof theInternet Appendixfor further detail). We report the responses for these subsets of bonds in Figure11, where the top (bottom) panels present results for oil supply (IST news) shocks.
  As predicted, the trough response of credit spreads is roughly 50% to 200% larger (depending on the specification) for high versus low leverage. In other words, bonds of firms with high leverage contribute most to the response of aggregate credit spreads to the macro shocks. For robustness, we similarly sort bonds based on credit ratings and in FigureIA.14report that the trough response of spreads to macro shocks is about 70% to 210% larger for speculative- versus investment-grade bonds. Consistent with the idea that high-leverage and speculative bonds have larger exposures to credit risk, we also see that long-term credit losses contribute substantially to the negative response of credit spreads for speculative-grade bonds and bonds of firms with high leverage. In contrast, the contribution of credit losses is virtually zero for low-leverage and investment-grade debt. More broadly, this evidence emphasizes the need to jointly model the response of corporate yield spreads and firm leverage to aggregate shocks, as in Gomes and Schmid (2021).
  Intermediaries: Recent intermediary asset pricing literature assigns an important role to financial intermediaries balance sheets in the origination and propagation of shocks (see He and Krishnamurthy (2013), Brunnermeier and Sannikov (2014)). Furthermore, Goldberg and Nozawa (2021) find that the balance sheet capacity of intermediaries is a key determinant of liquidity supply in OTC bond markets. To investigate whether macro shocks are propagated via intermediaries' balance sheet effects, we first augment the local linear projections of equations (3) and (4) with two measures of financial intermediary health, proposed in Adrian, Etula, and Muir (2014) and He, Kelly, and Manela (2017), respectively. These ratios predict returns in the time series in many asset classes, including corporate bonds (He, Kelly, and Manela (2017), Haddad and Muir (2021). Second, we analyze the response of the intermediary leverage ratios to macro shocks in these augmented projections. Controlling for intermediaries leverage does not alter the response ofand credit spreads (and) to macro shocks. The estimated impulse responses, reported in the top two rows of FigureIA.12, are almost indistinguishable from those in Figure3. Turning to the response of intermediary leverage, we see that the results depend on the type of shock. In the bottom row of FigureIA.12, we see that intermediary leverage ratios do not respond strongly to oil shocks, whereas they do respond to IST news shocks. Importantly, the response of the leverage ratios is delayed relative toanddoes notprecede the response of credit spreads. This evidence rules out a story whereby credit risk premia increase only after, and because, the macro shocks impact the balance sheets of financial intermediaries. Our results thus highlight the importance of pure macro shocks, although financial intermediaries' balance sheet positions, and illiquidity more generally, can certainly amplify such shocks.
  Debt Issuance: Recent literature suggests that firms issue too much credit when it is priced aggressively in a credit boom, which contributes to the economic bust when the elevated sentiment predictably reverts (Greenwood and Hanson (2013), López-Salido, Stein, and Zakrajšek (2017)). We show in the last row of FigureIA.13that corporate bond issuance does not respond to oil supply and IST news shocks. This finding is perhaps unsurprising in light of our previous results. First, we have already shown that the response of credit spreads to shocks is due mostly to time-varying risk premia rather than mispricing. Second, it seems unlikely that firm managers would immediately understand the information contained in these shocks, fully adjust their expectations (such that their forecast errors are unforecastable by the shocks), and arrange for a bond issue within a few periods. Indeed, we show in the previous subsection that the forecast errors of professional forecasters are predictable, which demonstrates that fully incorporating information from macro shocks is a nontrivial task that requires constant updating in an environment free of parameter or model uncertainty.
  The takeaway from this section is that a combination of information rigidities on the part of credit market participants and leverage-related amplifications are likely driving the response of credit spreads and credit risk premia to macro shocks. Since models that embed these two features do not exist, it is of great interest to explore the equilibrium pricing and leverage of firms making investment decisions in the face of aggregate shocks, while being subject to various forms of information rigidities.
article.V. External validity: Do Macro Shocks Le:
We argue that credit risk premia are strongly time-varying due to corporate bonds' exposures to fundamental economic shocks. While economic theory suggests that time-variation in expected returns is largely common across asset classes, existing evidence to that effect is limited (Cochrane (2011)). For instance, Chen, Collin-Dufresne, and Goldstein (2009) find that credit spreads, the most popular proxy for time-variation in credit risk premia, and the dividend-to-price ratio (), the most popular proxy for time-variation in equity risk premia, are much less correlated in the data than what is implied by a habit model.26Over their common sample, the unconditional correlation betweenandequals 0.46, whereas the correlation betweenandis a stunning −0.44. A multitude of factors may contribute to this unconditional disconnect, such as differences in cash flow dynamics, payout policy, sentiment, clientele effects, flight-to-liquidity, and so on. The shocks we study are exogenous and plausibly hold these confounding factors constant. Thus, impulse responses to these shocks represent a relatively powerful empirical test of the hypothesis that time-variation in risk premia in stock and bond markets is common.
  We proceed analogously to the case of credit markets, and in Figure12we present impulse responses to the exogenous oil supply, IST news, and defense spending news shocks for three measures of equity risk premia.27Our first measure is. While equity risk premia and dividend growth jointly determine, previous literature finds that shocks to dividend growth largely cancel out across stocks, such that most variation in the market'sis due to equity risk premia (e.g., Campbell (1991)). The second measure is a popular structural proxy for equity risk premia: risk aversion () implied by the habit model of Campbell and Cochrane (1999). In this model,is nearly linear in the surplus consumption ratio, which determines risk aversion.28Our final measure is the news implied volatility index () of Manela and Moreira (2017). This newspaper text-based measure of uncertainty predicts equity returns much like the VIX. In contrast to the VIX,is available over a much longer sample since 1890. Theandmeasures are particularly interesting to check for external validity because they are not based on prices likeand credit spreads.
  The results show that all three proxies for equity risk premia fall after positive macroeconomic shocks with similar significance and magnitude as for the case of credit spreads. After an oil supply shock, each proxy reaches its trough at a two- to three-year horizon and this trough impact (in standard deviation units) equals −0.15 forandand −0.20 for. The response to an IST news shock is relatively faster, as all three measures of equity risk premia trough in the first year. The trough impact is again large at about −0.15 forandand −0.25 for. We further see that the impact of a defense spending news shock is negative and significant in the long term forat about −0.06 standard deviations, similar to the result for credit spreads.also drops after a defense spending news shock, but it already troughs after six quarters.
  These findings provide external validity to the two main hypotheses studied in this paper. First, a negative macro shock causes a significant increase in risk premia. Second, risk premia peak only with a delay, which means that prices do not adjust fully and immediately to shocks, consistent with information rigidity.
article.VI. Conclusion:
Credit spreads respond countercyclically to oil supply, IST news, and defense spending news shocks. Because of their very nature, these macro shocks are unlikely to be correlated with lagged or contemporaneous credit market shocks. Considering that the shocks we study are distinct in terms of economic provenance, sample coverage, and identification approach, the evidence we present is remarkably consistent and robust. Through various decompositions of credit spreads and credit returns, we identify the channel of the transmission: macroeconomic fluctuations cause strong variation in corporate bond risk premia, with a relatively smaller impact on cash flows.
  More generally, we argue that corporate bonds are exposed to macro shocks through their default risk, and it is this exposure that gets compensated with a time-varying premium. While this link has been previously suggested in reduced-form regressions, the main contribution of this paper is to identify and quantify this channel with exogenous macro shocks. An additional contribution is to show that these same shocks impact equity risk premia in almost exactly the same way.
  We find that the large and protracted response of credit spreads is due to a combination of information rigidities on the part of credit market participants and leverage-related amplifications. Understanding the nature of the information rigidities is an interesting area for future research. Specifically, how do corporate bond market participants form expectations about default and credit risk premia in the face of large macroeconomic disturbances? The broad message of our paper is that more work is needed to understand how information rigidities affect the price and quantity of credit following macro shocks.
  Our results address a long-standing goal of macro finance, namely, to shed light on the channels through which fundamental shocks affect various sectors of the economy. The causal link we establish from the macroeconomy to credit markets contributes to the large body of recent empirical work that focuses almost exclusively on the macro impact of credit booms. Indeed, our evidence for “reverse causality” is surprisingly strong and implies a large potential feedback from the macroeconomy to credit, even when shocks originate in credit markets. Modeling the two-way interaction between the macroeconomy and credit markets and understanding its contribution to business cycles remains an important area for future research.
